hindi: மछली के सिर और अरबी का सूप

region_of_origin: China (Cantonese); widely popular in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fish soup food product' class; no specific entry for Fish Head Yam Soup exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers fish soup generically. No specific code for fish head yam soup variant exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue regional prepared soups at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Fish Head Yam Soup is approximately one bowl of 400 g, which includes broth, fish head pieces, yam chunks, and other accompaniments such as tomatoes and tofu.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Fish and Seafood Soups; USDA FoodData Central — Taro (cooked) and Fish Head (snapper, cooked) reference entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB or USDA entry exists for Fish Head Yam Soup as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore fish soup entries (milky fish soup, fish head soup) and USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked taro (FDC ID 170071) and cooked fish head (snapper). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these references and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from fish and evaporated milk fat fraction. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not reliably available for this composite preparation from approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Taro (cooked) and mixed fish soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Head Yam Soup. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on the GI of cooked taro (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate contributor. The protein and fat from fish and evaporated milk further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (53 × 23.2 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 12.3 g. Available carbohydrate per serving = (5.8 g/100 g × 400 g) × 1.0 = 23.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly 49% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per serving, request reduced salt during preparation, and avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce at the table; the evaporated milk component should also be minimised or replaced with low-fat milk to reduce overall sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Yam (taro) contributes a moderate carbohydrate load (5.8 g per 100 g; 23.2 g per 400 g serving) with a low estimated GI of 53, and the protein and fat from fish and evaporated milk further blunt the postprandial glycemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a standard 400 g serving as part of a balanced meal, but should monitor total carbohydrate intake from the yam component and pair the soup with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice to keep the overall meal glycemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: The dish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from the fish head and anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger, but the frying step and evaporated milk contribute saturated fat (estimated 1.4 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (280 mg per 100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a preparation where the fish head is simmered rather than deep-fried before adding to the broth, request reduced salt, and limit evaporated milk to minimise saturated fat and sodium intake while retaining the cardioprotective omega-3 benefits of the fish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish head provides collagen and omega-3 fatty acids; ginger contains gingerols with documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Head Yam Soup is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the fish and the gingerol compounds in ginger; sodium intake should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Head Yam Soup (鱼头芋头汤 / 魚頭芋頭湯; Yútóu Yùtóu Tāng) is a rich, comforting Cantonese soup dish that is a beloved staple in Singapore and Malaysia. The preparation begins by deep-frying the fish head — typically from a large freshwater or marine fish such as snapper or grass carp — until golden, which develops a deep umami flavour and produces the characteristic milky-white broth when the fried bones are simmered in water for an extended period. Evaporated milk is often added to enhance the creaminess and colour of the broth. Large chunks of yam (taro) are simmered in the broth until soft and partially dissolved, contributing a thick, velvety texture and a subtle earthy nuttiness. Supporting ingredients typically include fresh ginger slices, ripe tomatoes, sour plum (preserved plum), salted vegetables (ham choy), and soft tofu, each adding layers of flavour — sour, savoury, and umami. Nutritionally, the soup provides moderate protein (6.5 g per 100 g) from the fish, a low-to-moderate carbohydrate load (5.8 g per 100 g) primarily from the yam, and beneficial micronutrients including selenium (12.5 mcg), vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg), phosphorus (85 mg), and potassium (220 mg). The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 53), making it a relatively glycemia-friendly main dish when portion-controlled. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (280 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per standard 400 g serving), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fish head contributes omega-3 fatty acids and collagen, and ginger provides anti-inflammatory gingerols, making this soup nutritionally dense and therapeutically relevant for joint health. It is typically served as a centrepiece dish at family lunches and dinners.

confidence_reason: No single approved source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact entry for Fish Head Yam Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB milky fish soup entries and USDA cooked taro and fish head reference data. The input AI-generated values were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with these references. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked taro GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this composite Cantonese fish head yam soup preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. Saturated fat (1.4 g per 100 g) is estimated from the fat fraction of evaporated milk and fried fish head using USDA FoodData Central reference values for evaporated whole milk (FDC ID 173419) and cooked snapper. 3. GI value of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked taro (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in any approved source. 4. The input record listed a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (400g)' entries); only one default serving of 400 g is retained. 5. The input source cited ('National University of Singapore Department of Nutrition') is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2 and has been replaced with HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence basis. 6. Halal status is marked true as the dish contains fish and dairy with no pork or alcohol in the standard preparation; individual restaurant preparations may vary.
